questions.stem: Which feature is common to all viruses?
questions.explanation: All viruses have nucleic acid (DNA or RNA), a protein capsid, no cytoplasm and few or no enzymes.

questions.stem: The protein coat that surrounds a virus's genetic material is called the:
questions.explanation: A capsid made of protein encloses the viral nucleic acid.

questions.stem: Which of these do viruses lack?
questions.explanation: Viruses have no cytoplasm and few or no enzymes, unlike cells.

questions.stem: Compared with cells, the size of viruses is:
questions.explanation: A shared feature of viruses is their small, fixed size.

questions.stem: The genetic material of viruses may be:

options.text: DNA or RNA, single- or double-stranded

questions.explanation: Viruses are diverse: their genetic material may be RNA or DNA, either single- or double-stranded.

questions.scenario: Some viruses have an outer envelope while others do not.
questions.stem: Where does an enveloped virus obtain its envelope?
questions.explanation: Enveloped viruses are surrounded by host cell membrane; others are non-enveloped.

questions.stem: Which of these is used as an example of a virus in the syllabus?
questions.explanation: Virus examples include bacteriophage lambda, coronaviruses and HIV.

questions.stem: Viruses rely on a host cell for all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Viruses depend on the host for energy, nutrition, protein synthesis and other life functions; the protein sequence is coded by the virus's own genes.

questions.scenario: In the lytic cycle, a bacteriophage infects a bacterium.
questions.stem: How does the lytic cycle end?

options.text: The host cell bursts (lyses), releasing new viruses
The viral DNA stays dormant in the host genome
The virus becomes a living cell
The host cell divides normally forever

questions.explanation: In the lytic cycle, new virus particles are assembled and the host cell lyses, releasing them.

questions.stem: In the lysogenic cycle, the viral genetic material:

options.text: Integrates into the host DNA and can remain dormant
Turns the virus into a bacterium

questions.explanation: In the lysogenic cycle the viral DNA integrates into the host genome and can be replicated with it while remaining dormant.

questions.stem: Which virus is used as the example for both the lytic and lysogenic cycles?
questions.explanation: Bacteriophage lambda is the example used for the phases of both the lytic and lysogenic cycles.

questions.stem: The great diversity of viruses suggests that they:

options.text: All arose from a single virus
Are all descended from bacteria only

questions.explanation: The diversity of viruses suggests several possible origins rather than a single common one.

questions.scenario: All viruses share an extreme form of obligate parasitism.
questions.stem: The structural features viruses have in common could therefore be regarded as an example of:
questions.explanation: Shared features arising from the same parasitic lifestyle, despite different origins, are an example of convergent evolution.

questions.stem: What do viruses and living organisms share, indicating a link between them?
questions.explanation: The genetic code is shared between viruses and living organisms.

questions.stem: Which two viruses are used as examples of rapid evolution?
questions.explanation: The syllabus uses influenza viruses and HIV as examples of rapid viral evolution.

questions.scenario: A rapidly evolving virus keeps changing its surface proteins.
questions.stem: What is a consequence for treating the disease it causes?

options.text: Vaccines and treatments may quickly become less effective
The disease becomes impossible to catch

questions.explanation: Rapid evolution means treatments and vaccines can lose effectiveness as the virus changes, complicating disease control.

questions.stem: Which statement about enzymes in viruses is correct?

options.text: Viruses have few or no enzymes of their own
Viruses have a full set of metabolic enzymes
Viruses use enzymes made of nucleic acid only

questions.explanation: Viruses have few or no enzymes, relying on the host cell's machinery.

questions.scenario: HIV uses RNA as its genetic material.
questions.stem: This shows that viruses:

options.text: Can use RNA rather than DNA

questions.explanation: Viral diversity includes the use of RNA, as in HIV, as the genetic material.

questions.stem: Why do some viruses evolve very rapidly?

options.text: High mutation rates and very fast reproduction
They reproduce only once per year

questions.explanation: Rapid evolution results from high mutation rates combined with very rapid reproduction in the host.

questions.stem: During the lytic cycle, new viral proteins are made using the:
questions.explanation: Because viruses lack their own protein-synthesis machinery, they use the host cell's ribosomes.

questions.scenario: A dormant viral genome integrated in a bacterium is later triggered by stress.
questions.stem: What is likely to happen next?

options.text: It switches to the lytic cycle and produces new viruses
It permanently leaves the cell without replicating
It stops the bacterium from ever dividing

questions.explanation: A prophage can switch from the lysogenic to the lytic cycle, producing and releasing new virus particles.

questions.stem: Which combination lists features shared by all viruses?

options.text: Small fixed size, nucleic acid, capsid, no cytoplasm

questions.explanation: All viruses share a small fixed size, nucleic acid genome, protein capsid, no cytoplasm and few/no enzymes.

questions.stem: Coronaviruses are given as an example to illustrate viral:
questions.explanation: Coronaviruses, alongside bacteriophage lambda and HIV, are examples showing the diversity of virus structure.

questions.stem: Obligate parasitism in viruses means they:

options.text: Can only reproduce inside a host cell
Do not need a host at all

questions.explanation: Viruses are obligate parasites, unable to reproduce without exploiting a host cell.

questions.scenario: The influenza virus changes slightly each year.
questions.stem: Why must the flu vaccine be updated regularly?

options.text: The virus evolves rapidly, changing its surface antigens

questions.explanation: Because influenza evolves rapidly, its antigens change and vaccines must be updated to remain effective.

questions.stem: Which is the correct general order of events in a lytic cycle?

options.text: Attachment, entry of nucleic acid, replication and assembly, lysis

questions.explanation: The lytic cycle proceeds by attachment, injection of nucleic acid, replication and assembly of new viruses, then lysis of the host.

questions.stem: The observation that different viruses have very different genome types supports the idea that viruses:

options.text: Evolved from other organisms on more than one occasion
Share a single recent common ancestor

questions.explanation: Their diversity is evidence for several separate origins of viruses from other organisms.

questions.stem: Why is the number of features shared by ALL viruses relatively small?

options.text: Viruses are highly diverse, likely with several origins
Viruses share all features with bacteria

questions.explanation: Because viruses are so diverse, only a few features (size, nucleic acid, capsid, no cytoplasm, few enzymes) are shared by all.

questions.stem: A key difference between the lysogenic and lytic cycles is that in the lysogenic cycle the virus:

options.text: Does not immediately destroy the host cell
Cannot enter the host at all

questions.explanation: In the lysogenic cycle the viral DNA is maintained in the host without immediately causing lysis.

questions.scenario: HIV mutates rapidly during an infection.
questions.stem: Why does this make treatment difficult?

options.text: The immune system easily clears it

questions.explanation: Rapid mutation in HIV allows drug-resistant variants to evolve, making treatment challenging.

questions.stem: Two viruses differ in whether their genetic material is single- or double-stranded. This illustrates:

options.text: That all viruses are the same

questions.explanation: Variation in strandedness of the genome is one aspect of the structural diversity of viruses.

questions.stem: Viruses can exist with very few genes mainly because they:

options.text: Use the host cell's machinery for most functions

questions.explanation: Relying on the host for energy, protein synthesis and other functions lets viruses survive with very few genes.

questions.stem: A bacteriophage is a virus that infects:
questions.explanation: A bacteriophage, such as lambda, is a virus that infects bacteria.

questions.stem: Similar capsid shapes evolving in unrelated viruses adapted to the same lifestyle is best explained by:
questions.explanation: Shared features from a shared parasitic mode of existence, despite separate origins, indicate convergent evolution.

questions.stem: A non-enveloped virus differs from an enveloped virus in that it:

options.text: Lacks a surrounding layer of host membrane

questions.explanation: Enveloped viruses carry a layer of host membrane; non-enveloped viruses do not.

questions.stem: Rapid viral evolution is a direct consequence of which combination?

options.text: Short generation times and high mutation rates
Long generation times and low mutation rates
The absence of any genetic material

questions.explanation: Very rapid reproduction and high mutation rates together drive rapid evolution in some viruses.

questions.stem: Why can a virus not carry out the lytic cycle on its own outside a cell?

options.text: It needs the host's energy and machinery to replicate

questions.explanation: Viruses lack the machinery for independent replication and depend entirely on a host cell.

questions.stem: That the genetic code is shared between viruses and cellular life suggests:

options.text: Viruses are connected to cellular life, likely originating from it
Viruses use a completely different code

questions.explanation: The shared genetic code links viruses to cellular life and supports origins from other organisms.

questions.stem: Which of these structures would you NOT expect to find in any virus?
questions.explanation: Viruses have no organelles such as mitochondria; they possess only a nucleic acid genome and a protein capsid.

questions.stem: The wide variation in virus shape and structure is described in the syllabus as viruses being:
questions.explanation: Viruses are highly diverse in their shape and structure.

questions.scenario: A newly emerged influenza strain spreads because people have little immunity to it.
questions.stem: This lack of immunity is a consequence of the virus's:
questions.explanation: Rapid evolution changes surface antigens, so existing immunity may not recognise the new strain.

questions.stem: When viral DNA is integrated into the host chromosome during the lysogenic cycle, it is copied:

options.text: Each time the host cell divides
By the virus's own enzymes only

questions.explanation: Integrated viral DNA is replicated along with the host genome each time the host cell divides.

questions.stem: The genetic material of a virus is best described as:

options.text: Either DNA or RNA, but not both
Always both DNA and RNA together

questions.explanation: A virus's genome is nucleic acid, either DNA or RNA, but never both at once.

questions.stem: Which best summarises current thinking on the origin of viruses?

options.text: They likely arose several times from other organisms
They all descend from one ancient virus

questions.explanation: The diversity of viruses points to several separate origins from other organisms.

questions.stem: Which life function can a virus NOT perform independently?
questions.explanation: Viruses cannot synthesise proteins independently; they rely on the host cell's ribosomes.

questions.stem: HIV, bacteriophage lambda and coronaviruses are grouped together in the syllabus to illustrate:

options.text: How much viruses vary in structure

questions.explanation: These examples are used to demonstrate the diversity of virus structure.

questions.stem: A practical consequence of rapid viral evolution for public health is that:

options.text: New vaccines or drugs may be needed repeatedly

questions.explanation: Because rapidly evolving viruses change, ongoing development of new vaccines and drugs is often needed.

questions.scenario: A student argues that viruses are alive because they contain DNA.
questions.stem: Why is this argument insufficient?

options.text: Containing nucleic acid does not make something self-sustaining

questions.explanation: Having nucleic acid is not enough; viruses lack the ability to carry out life processes independently, so they are non-living.

questions.stem: A virus in the lysogenic cycle whose DNA is integrated into the host genome is often called a:
questions.explanation: Integrated viral DNA in a bacterial host during the lysogenic cycle is known as a prophage.

questions.stem: Convergent evolution among viruses would produce:

options.text: Similar features in viruses with different origins
Viruses that never share any features

questions.explanation: Convergent evolution gives rise to similar features in organisms (or viruses) of different origins facing similar pressures.
